speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape three, with Mr. Sam Mathis, February the 20th, 1996, in Los Angeles, California, United States of America. So Mr. Mathis, you were describing how you worked at Skarzysko. Tell us what happened then.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then, in Skarzysko, they make, a long time to call up, everybody going to come in the lot. And they said, everything, to try again. Some to leave a while and some to take out to Skarzysko. I was on the lot. A day before, two days before, I find out as they come, and they said, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] also come women, to work with the team.

speaker: interviewer
text: They were going to select people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I did, I know this. I took the lining from [INAUDIBLE] jacket. And I make me a blouse. I took [INAUDIBLE] from a light, make me a collar, to look young. And I come. They said, everybody out. Everybody out. I was dressed, then the Gestapo men, the oberscharfuhrer there, he said, oh, you're woman. You go back.

speaker: interviewee
text: See? That's that. No, that's not happened. Still happened, something else. Going back over there, comes around a Jewish policeman, comes in a policeman. Go out! I said, why you sent me out, back? The Gestapo sends me in. And you send me out? Why you send me out, to go to hell? To go into death?

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, go out! The Jewish policeman. I did it. I cannot do nothing with him. You see, police is police. The same that is going on every place. Now I got a mistake. See, it was already two lines. One to death, one stays. One to death, one stays.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was a fool. I go to the death. What can I do? You know, they're bombing [NON-ENGLISH]. [INAUDIBLE] you, that place! What you going to do? What you doing? I said, what I'm going to do? Then I went to go out. The Gestapo said, stay here.

speaker: interviewee
text: I watch him. When he  turns his head, I jump out. Go back to the officer. And I said, you know, [GERMAN] in Deutch, and I told him.

speaker: interviewee
text: You told me to go in the barrack. The Jewish officer send me back. Go back. Then he lost me there. Then I was out, back alive. See? I fight. You never going to believe it. That's the truth, that sometime I've met the Jewish-- because they send me out back? For what? Let me ask you something. You said that the girls called you [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, they call me [NON-ENGLISH] they call me [NON-ENGLISH] It's easy for them to call me [NON-ENGLISH], Schulum Moishe. In the shul they now say that they call me [NON-ENGLISH]. In the lager, they used to call me [NON-ENGLISH]. What a difference. Both names is my name. I took over [INAUDIBLE], well, they're both my name. I show, I got the-- the man in Poland. He said, [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened then, when you jumped out of the line?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, jumped off the line, then they send me back to the beginning of the line, to save life, and then left over. I stay alive. See? After all, they looked for the clothes later on. Later on. That's it. And took me away from over there, and took her from this place. It's [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of this place again?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sulejow [NON-ENGLISH] They make it over there, pentagram. You know, the German calls pentagram. They built big ditches against the [INAUDIBLE] go through this. And this, over there, I work. And they saw me. They told me for me to work, is work. They said, you know that if you want, you tell them to make it smooth, this and this good.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then I told you before, then again, in London, from the Ukrainian, the Gestapo, they said, you need a tailor. And the policeman, [NON-ENGLISH], is from [INAUDIBLE]. They ask him, which is a good tailor here?

speaker: interviewee
text: He told them about me. You know, [INAUDIBLE], they took me away from the work. And I sitting and work. And work, fine. Rather than dig the ditches. That's it. And he helped me out.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you actually worked as a tailor for the German soldiers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Men like this, on the fields. For them, for the Gestapo. Not for the whole of them, just for the Gestapo and Poland men. He can help a lot. One man can help you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, Gestapo names. Who'd name the name? Well, I'll name the name, Ukrainian, Ukrainian. He can talk Polish, everything. Pole.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me how you go to Sulejow?

speaker: interviewee
text: To Sulejow? They took us with the train.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did it take you to get there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not long. Not long. I don't know, maybe a day. I don't know how long. I don't remember this very long.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember when this was? What time of year?

speaker: interviewee
text: In summertime. It was summertime.

speaker: interviewer
text: Of what year?

speaker: interviewee
text: Before, when they close off the [INAUDIBLE]. I don't know. When they took us, I don't know. Before this must be maybe, in '44, beginning '44, maybe. '44.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were the conditions like in Sulejow?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, no good. You used to sleep in a barn. That's total, a barn, is total. You sleep, that's it. That's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about the food?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, food, food, bad food. The food they  give you, just so you can work. That's it. Nothing. Not you can work. You got no choice. You work. You have to work. Give me no food, that's it. Don't ask questions with the food. They never give you enough. Never enough. This is the truth. Everybody's going to tell you the same thing. They don't give you, never enough.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how long were you in Sulejow?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not long. Until they finish this. Than they send me from Sulejow. From Sulejow they went to Buchenwald. No, no, from Sulejow, to Tschenstochau. To Tschenstochau.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you find out you were going to Tschenstochau?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't find out nothing until they took  me there. I don't know where they send me. Every place they just put on the train. You never know where you go.

speaker: interviewer
text: Maybe you go to the gas chamber. You never know where you're going. You don't ask questions. For what? Who's going to answer this? Nobody. You ask questions of the German, where you take me? Take me for party? Take me for party? I wish it would happen. Take me to death. Where are you going to death? To use you up until you die. That's it. So how did you get to Tschenstochau?

speaker: interviewee
text: With the train, they take me there.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did that take?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh no, I can never-- You think I pay attention, how long they take me? Never. Other people, they tell you the date? How can you tell them how long? Some of [INAUDIBLE] are still alive. That's it. That I can tell you, not better.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what do you remember about Tschenstochau?

speaker: interviewee
text: Tschenstochau? The train take me. What was the work I did in Tschenstochau? I forgot, even, the work I did over there. Yeah, they were with barracks.

speaker: interviewee
text: What kind of work I did? I don't know even what kind of work I did. I don't know. I'll tell you, I don't remember the work I did over there. Why don't we start with describing the barracks? Tell me what they looked like.

speaker: interviewee
text: What?

speaker: interviewer
text: In Tschenstochau, the barracks, what were they like?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Tschenstochau barracks? No, they change it over a long time. They give you dirt, and then they make barracks. What I'm working? I didn't work for a tailor there. I don't know what kind of work I did. I don't know. But I was there not very long. No, not long.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you with anyone while you were in Tschenstochau?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, lots. I met a lot of people from Miedzyrzecz. I met over there-- I used to go to the women's barrack. Then I found my-- she's my wife.

speaker: interviewer
text: How come you went to the women's barracks?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, everybody did. Because you've got to go. Just you cannot go in ever at night. Go just in daytime, to look around. You see, you are afraid they see you. Oh yeah, you was afraid.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you met your future wife there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you meet her?

speaker: interviewee
text: I come over there. I don't know. I don't know how. Then I see next to her was one woman, she was not far from our city, about 12 miles from our city. And I know her daughter. I showed that picture of the daughter, here. She left me a picture for her daughter. And I talk. A talk to her. Her name was, I think, [NON-ENGLISH]. I don't know. Then I talk. And I see she works. And I said-- I was talking to her. And I got, from another woman, next to me, she's from Lodz, I think. She's dead already. And I see she worked with the work. And I went this time, over there, the same Ukrainian or German, I don't know what he was. Well, he heard me and [INAUDIBLE]. He was over there too. So he know I'm a tailor already. He helped me out of there. You go over there. Used to come the trains, with [INAUDIBLE] and other things, I can take it. He let me take it. Then I see my wife over there. She goes to work. Works in the night. I said to her, you know, because she has the father's brother there. I said, I can help you.

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, OK. I help her. I say, I can help you, I think. I can help you out a little bit. What's wrong with this?

words.text: I can help you, I think.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, I help her out. I never figure I'm going to marry her. I don't know nothing. I just help her.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I help her and her uncle and the other, the cousins. I don't care. I've got it. I can help you. Why not?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then as she went to work, I make her [INAUDIBLE]. I'm back home-- came back. Then I got somebody, [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a shoemaker. I talk to him. I say, she go barefoot and this. I said, you know, over there, and she needs some. Can you make me a pair of shoes for her? He made them.

words.text: you make me a pair of shoes

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know how he did it. He did it. He did it. And I give her the shoes. Then late night sometime, then they took me away from there too. Not very long, we're there. A long time it was happening. But there were too some the girls, with a little boy. So used to come over there, a train, with food, with potatoes, other things. And these boys went to this train, they stole potatoes. And they caught him. They caught him. And they caught him. The same man that I know, he caught him, the Gestapo man. Now, I see who takes him, the [NON-ENGLISH]. You know, coming towards me. I don't know how  they call a [INAUDIBLE]. Wherever they take him, he would never got out alive. I went to him and I said, let him go. Let him go. He said to me in Polish, [POLISH], you son of a bitch. You [POLISH], son of a bitch. I'm not going to let him go.

words.text: I see who takes him, the

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, well, let him go. Take me. I said to him, in German, take me. He said, no. Then I said to him, I tried to talk to him. I said to him, the Yiddish boy? I some Jewish boy, [NON-ENGLISH]. You know what [NON-ENGLISH]. [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewee
text: To run away. Yeah, run away. And I talk with this one-- [NON-ENGLISH]. Just take me now. Now, to take me. No, they say, you're not-- let go. With that, the boy would be dead. They take him out. I helped him out. I tell you. If I can, I would do it. I did it. A lot of things like this. Yes. The same thing. Later on, they took me away from them, I ignore them. I rode on the truck. And then they took him away to Buchenwald. On the truck, they took him away. And I said, oh boy, I never see her, she works now. And they took her away. Then I passed the place where she works. I see a woman. Now, there's some money in my pocket. I throw it and I said, give it to Dora. I don't know what's happened. [INAUDIBLE] that's it. [INAUDIBLE], took me away from here. I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let me just ask you before we go any further--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah?

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of your future wife? Can you tell us?

speaker: interviewee
text: Dora, Dora, Dora.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how long were you in Tschenstochau?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not very long. No, they took her, not long. A few months. I don't know. Not long.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you left, do you remember what time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because the Russian come closer. You see, that what they done. Because the Russian come closer. They didn't take her away because they want to. They got no choice. They got no choice. Some people, they left. In place what my wife's going to tell you later, she was in [NON-ENGLISH]. They were liberated over there. They never got time to take them away from there. They took us before. See, they took me to Buchenwald. To suffer more a little bit. That's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what time of year it was when you were--

speaker: interviewee
text: That's it. They took us to Buchenwald-- I got it. I think it was in-- what did I told you? Beginning, '45. No, '44. '44.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember, was it spring or summer?

speaker: interviewee
text: Summer. This is summertime. No, I think it was in-- no, not summer. It was, I think, it was in fall. I'm afraid. I don't know. It was wintertime.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it getting cold already?

speaker: interviewee
text: Fall, it's fall time, before summer. Summer, we were liberated.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how did you get to Buchenwald from Tschenstochau?

speaker: interviewee
text: A train, train. They take me on train, over there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember that trip?

speaker: interviewee
text: Trip? Trip to death. Trip to death. What kind of trip is this? A nice trip, when you think, Las Vegas?

speaker: interviewer
text: A trip? A trip? It's a bad trip. A death trip. A funeral trip. You never know where you're going.

speaker: interviewee
text: That trip, funeral trip. Oh boy, I wish I would know. But don't know nothing. Some people, they liberate, took them in-- they jumped. Not to Buchenwald. They jumped on the trains before. I forget. A lot of things I missed, I cannot remember. Comes later, it's too late to tell you.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember about arriving at Buchenwald?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, over there, they tell you the same thing. Put with the-- I think now, it wasn't fall. It was cold, very cold. They transferred you.

speaker: interviewer
text: After that, they take out everything. It was in a-- how do you call? like, a swimming pool, a [NON-ENGLISH] terrible. I figure after this, I'm going to the gas chamber.

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't know myself. I didn't know if I'm going to work. I figure I'm going to the gas chamber. They took us to a barrack.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they give you clothes to wear?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, oh, well, the clothes. [NON-ENGLISH], how you call, the stripes pattern. Yeah, striped.

speaker: interviewer
text: Striped uniform?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, striped. Yeah. That's what they give you, nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have shoes?

speaker: interviewee
text: Holland has-- how you call it? Like ladies wear sometimes shoes?

speaker: interviewer
text: You mean, like, wooden clogs?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, wooden clogs, that's it. Holland's. Yes. That's what's happened. That's it. Often over there, they took you all the time, a lot of time to [NON-ENGLISH] out, for work, the ruins, after the bomb. The ruins, I was working over there. Out. Because I still-- no, you-- I was afraid you can't take nothing. If you take nothing, you go in, they take you right over there to the gas chamber over there, right away. I was afraid to take nothing. I don't take. I was careful with this. I think, maybe I'm going to get over. [INAUDIBLE] kilometers. That's happened same when I was in Buchenwald. They make a selection. They take out of other places to work. Then same, a father and a brother. Father and a son with others. And they take my brother last to take my place. But they take me [NON-ENGLISH]. Their place was [INAUDIBLE]. And father, someplace else, and son, someplace else. Then the father asked me, maybe if I want to go with them, and he want to go to my place. I said, oh, no, no. That's my luck. Mine. My [NON-ENGLISH]. If I die, I die. If I live, I live. I don't change nothing. That is my [NON-ENGLISH]. [NON-ENGLISH] is gone, everybody. And I was working. I was lucky. Lucky, still not lucky. Still lucky I was [INAUDIBLE] 1945, that's when I was lucky, not before. If I was lucky, I'd be there. The train was lucky.

speaker: interviewer
text: So while you were in Buchenwald, did you actually work at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: Work-- I don't know. There's something. Oh, yeah. I [INAUDIBLE] clean up. That's is all the work.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they take you out during the day and bring you back at night?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes. Bring you back. Appells, appells. You know, to count you're back.

speaker: interviewer
text: If you're right and if you don't run away. I mean, working with the Germans, they would kill anyway. Where you going to run? See, a Jewish son, where you going to run?

speaker: interviewee
text: Where you're going is no good. Every place is kaput. I know these things. Good.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you with anyone that you knew while you were in Buchenwald?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Buchenwald? No. It was strangers all. I know when later on, I find out, as my wife, they took her away in another camp. And my wife said a cousin was in Buchenwald too. I find it out. And I was in Tschenstochau. I helped them out. And I find out these were the children, and he can help me out. Somebody was coming, from another-- the boys, the young, the children, that they feed better than all of them.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when you come to them in camp, I said, you know, they're [NON-ENGLISH]. He said, [NON-ENGLISH]? Yes. I said, tell him I'm here.

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe he can help me too. Never happened. Never. I was good when I there.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you in Buchenwald?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, Buchenwald? Until they take me to-- no, they make selection to take people to work out. And still, and we still go. Every morning, they took out people to the gas chamber. Or one, he still was alive. Shove them in and everything, like this, when they were still alive. They always took them every night and day. Every night and day.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see the ovens working?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes. You can see the smoke goes up, every night and day. You still was alive and they run. What can you do? You see yourself. You never know what's going to be now with you.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your health like at Buchenwald?

speaker: interviewee
text: My health? I was lucky I was still alive. I don't know my health. You got a doctor? Ask him. You was afraid to even tell them you sick. You so afraid. You go and tell you sick, you was kaput, you're gone. If you tell you're sick, it's death. You never tell nobody. You don't open your mouth with this. Never. You're sick. You're sick.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh then, OK, you want to know when they took me to Colditz? To Colditz, then I saw, again, other things. Colditz, I started to work in a factory, to build a new factory, for a new mission.

speaker: interviewee
text: He wanted to start, again, the war over. He is already almost kaput, almost straight to hell. He's still dead.

speaker: interviewee
text: Just nothing shake him. He longed to still go. I work in a factory. [INAUDIBLE] you know? Prisoners, all in there. Dutch prisoners?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Oh, they lost very good people, very good people. Over there, still was the same Ukrainian with me, too, or some. No, some people, them the war was already close. Closer, closer, closer. Then I don't know how long I was over there. Good thing it was not very long there. We died over there. It was the hard labor, over there. They make a new factory. So hard. You work hard.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of work?

speaker: interviewee
text: Build a new factory. Cement work. I worked in cement. I'm a cement man? What can you do? They said, the Jewish can do nothing. They do everything. You got no choice.

speaker: interviewer
text: Everything, what you can. If you know how to do it, you do it. You know? Where were you living while you were at Colditz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Colditz? In the barracks, two barracks. And I laid down in the night, later on, far later, start up with bombing oh, every night. Bombs and bombs, war, oh that's terrible. Now it's kaput. They don't know if I'm not there. You can be bombed at the same factory and make them dead, all of them. Then they decided [NON-ENGLISH]. To run away from factory. It comes closer, closer, and we going to take it. I figure I want to hide myself.

speaker: interviewee
text: I figure this. I got a place ready. And it's a chimney. Over there, to put inside. And later, I said, what I'm going to do?

speaker: interviewee
text: If they coming in, the Russians, somebody, it's OK. But if comes in a German, he's going to kill me before. I don't do nothing. I'm not going-- leave it to my luck. If I've still got luck, maybe farther. I don't do it. I try. Because my mind said, don't do it. You did it so far. Do it again. And I did it. Then is the start of the death march. Oh, the death march. Oh boy, oh boy, the death march. This is terrible. So many people, you kill in a day. And I think of myself. I was like this. Like this. I cannot drag mine foot no more. Never. I can't move my feet like this.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said to this Gestapo man, and I started to talk to him. You know what? I'm a tailor. I used for the Gestapo. Used to work for them. And he's, you are a tailor man? Yes. Yes. You know what I'm going to do for you? He was good to me. Can you tailor my things? I said, can I tailor your things? Like a Muselmann? I'm going to do it. What can I do? Dragging around. Why not? He go, the only thing you can do for me, a favor. I got my friend. Can I take it too, with me?

speaker: interviewee
text: The name was [NON-ENGLISH], a boy. I said, I want him to help me to do this. Look. OK, he give me the other men to help you to carry the stuff in. And he let them see that I help him. Then we going on the way. All the time it was one man, old men, the Gestapo. He was from Majdanek. He was a murderer. Every time you said, [NON-ENGLISH], Shoot him. [NON-ENGLISH], shoot him.

speaker: interviewee
text: The only time we stopped in the night, we stopped in a barn to sleep. And two man hide under the straw. Straw? Straw? Straw? It's straw, yeah? I don't know. I hope so. Yeah? They hide under this. And the German come down and said, somebody took two Jews, hiding over there.

speaker: interviewee
text: They took them out. They kill them both. Both right away.

speaker: interviewer
text: In front of you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yeah, in front of everybody to see, not to run away no more, To show everybody what they can do. There was no run away. They kill every person. You cannot walk no more? Shoot. The line was going to from Colditz, all of them, Jews. Go on the street. You see the Germans said, we don't know nothing. We passed so many so many little towns. The Germans was on the [INAUDIBLE]. He was-- [SPITTING] --by the fence. Used to do like this. They said, we don't know nothing. They knew everything. What they said is all lies. They knew everything, But they don't--

speaker: interviewer
text: When you walked through any of these towns and you saw the citizens, did anyone ever try to help you, try to give anyone some food?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. You know, the Russians, later on, the airplanes used to throw [NON-ENGLISH]. How you call, papers? How you call it in English?

speaker: interviewer
text: Fliers.

speaker: interviewee
text: Fliers. You know, to the Germans to put away the guns. I mean, you can see the plane over us, over us. And they do not-- because they see who we are. And the Germans is dragging in the ditches. They let us go like this. Or they come back. And they will find a way. I've seen they are close. We cannot do nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the weather like when you were marching?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was warm, summer, already, the spring. Come to spring, it's already. Oh, you see, a lot of people more-- And so many in the fields and berm. I never can explain so many-- walking, and walking so much, so far from Colditz, to Czechoslovakia, to walk all the time. Terrible.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long during the day did you actually walk?

speaker: interviewee
text: You walk until you  can't. In the night, some people run away, in the night, in the woods, in the woods. You know? In the [INAUDIBLE], there's woods now. They run away. I walked in the woods too. I figured. Still, I was afraid. I was frightened man. My convictions said, don't do it. You've had a life [INAUDIBLE]. There you go, and I didn't do it. I think they get us to Theresienstadt, Czechoslovakia. Over there, we're going by there was another camp other there. We went past them. They say, hi! Hi! You know, people over there too. [INAUDIBLE] was ready for them too. They took us over there in a basement, a temple basement. Sitting, all night, and crying, cry. We was looking. Nothing happened. In the morning-- [KNOCKING] [NON-ENGLISH]! [NON-ENGLISH]. Everybody, life. They took us out. The Russians do that very good. I don't care what America talk about the Russian. The Russians did that better than the American. I'm going to tell what's happened. It's not happening, over. They did very good. They took us out. They dress us. And take us, and we take a bath. They took a German woman to give us the bath. They dress us, everything. The German woman. Then after all, took us to the gas chamber to show us the gas chamber. [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: You never see this. A corridor. What your corridor called? Goes like this, like a snake. Like a snake.

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] never know nothing. [INAUDIBLE] has never know nothing, was going like a snake. Goes like this. That was the camp, was the gas chamber. Around them, was wire link. And over the top from the wire link was glass, cutting glass. You can never run away no place. That's the gas chamber. That, as I said, that's a new place. [INAUDIBLE] for you to take it. Long day is over with us. One day.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, when you arrived to Theresienstadt, where exactly did they take you when you arrived?

speaker: interviewee
text: I told you, in the basement. In the temple basement.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so you were only there one night before the Russians came?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this was when?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know when. We were right before this. There we were. It was right before they liberate us now. I think one night. I don't remember, I mean, what I went through. I know I never went out from the place, the same place. All over was the same, crying, crying, and crying. That's when they play the music. Maybe two nights. I don't remember this for sure. I tell you the truth. I remember I was over there. And they liberate us.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you remember feeling when the Russians arrived?

speaker: interviewee
text: What do you think I was feeling? What'd you feel? Very good. Well, still everything is nothing. You're alone. Alone. Nobody.

speaker: interviewer
text: How you feeling? You're still alone. This is not your country. This is still Czechoslovakia. You got nobody there. Nobody. Nowhere to go.

speaker: interviewee
text: Go here or go there. To [INAUDIBLE]. This was after camp. [NON-ENGLISH] That's it. [NON-ENGLISH] Europe.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Russian [INAUDIBLE]. Get a nice place to live, yeah, some get food, get housing, had to rest, everything. Then they decided after a few months, I show the picture. Did you see the picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: For me they decided every Polish citizen got to go back to Poland. I said, I'm not going to the anti-Semitic Poland. I got nobody left there. I don't need it, Poland. I said, I'm not going back to Poland. Then decided on a transfer to make it to not to Germany, to Austria, To Salzburg. They transferred us in. They make me go to Salzburg, to Austria. They made me [INAUDIBLE], transport, [INAUDIBLE] some Jews, two guys, it was something, jumped down from the train. And they said, they run, some German. Took away two pair of shoes and come back to the train. Then the American said, no, I'm going to take you back to Czechoslovakia. See, Americans, did nothing. You know, the Russians did something else. They said, give us 24 hours free to do with a German you can do it.

speaker: interviewee
text: What can I do? Then I take five of them. My friend, I see some German boy drive a bike. I said, [NON-ENGLISH], because he was German. Take the bike. I break the bike in pieces. He cries. OK. Let's stop right there. We'll change the tape.

words.text: Then I take five of them.

speaker: interviewee
text: All the things, comes late, to tell you. Other things happened. Yeah, I can talk now.
